content.text: User Profiles & Preferences

An agent that remembers who it is talking to feels qualitatively different from one that starts fresh every session. User profiles are structured, persistent records of what an agent knows about each individual: their stated preferences, conversational patterns, domain expertise, and inferred working style. Injecting a compact profile into every prompt lets the agent tailor its tone, depth, and format without asking the same questions repeatedly.
Profile Layers

attrs.alt: User profile layers: explicit, learned, and inferred — and the update lifecycle

content.text: A well-designed user profile has three layers, each populated by a different mechanism:
Layer
Source
Examples
Update trigger
Explicit
User provides directly
Name, timezone, language, role
Onboarding form or /set command
Learned
Extracted from conversation turns
Prefers concise summaries, uses Python, works in fintech
Automatic after each turn
Inferred
Derived from usage patterns
Asks detailed questions → high expertise; asks in evenings → late-shift worker
Periodic batch job
The UserProfile Dataclass

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class UserProfile:
    user_id: str

    # Explicit fields — set at onboarding or by user command
    name:        str  = ""
    language:    str  = "en"
    timezone:    str  = "UTC"
    role:        str  = ""
    team:        str  = ""

    # Learned fields — updated from conversation
    communication_style: str = "balanced"  # "concise" | "detailed" | "balanced"
    preferred_format:    str = "prose"     # "prose" | "bullet" | "table"
    expertise_level:     str = "intermediate"  # "beginner" | "intermediate" | "expert"
    domain_focus:        list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # e.g. ["python", "finance"]
    tool_preferences:    dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)  # e.g. {"ide": "vscode"}

    # Inferred fields — set by analytics
    active_hours:    list[int] = field(default_factory=list)  # hours of day (0–23)
    topic_interests: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    # Metadata
    created_at:   str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    updated_at:   str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    turn_count:   int = 0

    def to_prompt_block(self) -> str:
        """Render as a compact system-prompt injection."""
        lines = [f"[USER PROFILE — {self.user_id}]"]
        if self.name:
            lines.append(f"  Name: {self.name}")
        lines.append(f"  Language: {self.language} | Timezone: {self.timezone}")
        if self.role:
            lines.append(f"  Role: {self.role}" + (f" @ {self.team}" if self.team else ""))
        lines.append(f"  Style: {self.communication_style}, Format: {self.preferred_format}, Level: {self.expertise_level}")
        if self.domain_focus:
            lines.append(f"  Domain focus: {', '.join(self.domain_focus[:5])}")
        if self.tool_preferences:
            prefs = ", ".join(f"{k}={v}" for k, v in list(self.tool_preferences.items())[:4])
            lines.append(f"  Tools: {prefs}")
        return "\n".join(lines)
Persisting Profiles
Profiles must survive process restarts. A JSONL file works for single-user deployments; a relational table scales to multi-user systems.
Automatic Preference Extraction

Rather than asking users to fill in a form, the agent can infer preferences by running a lightweight extraction pass after each turn. A fast model reads the conversation excerpt and returns a small JSON patch to apply to the profile.
Injecting the Profile Into Each Prompt

The profile is injected as a block near the top of the system prompt, before the conversation history but after the main instructions. Keeping it compact (under 150 tokens) avoids crowding out other context.

def build_system_prompt(
    base_instructions: str,
    profile: UserProfile,
) -> str:
    profile_block = profile.to_prompt_block()
    return f"""{base_instructions}

{profile_block}

Adapt your response style, depth, and examples to match the user's profile above.
If the profile shows expertise_level=expert, skip basic definitions.
If preferred_format=bullet, default to bullet lists unless prose is clearer.
If communication_style=concise, keep responses under 200 words unless the topic requires more.
""".strip()


def chat_turn(
    user_id: str,
    user_message: str,
    history: list[dict],
    base_instructions: str,
) -> str:
    profile  = load_profile(user_id)
    system   = build_system_prompt(base_instructions, profile)
    messages = history + [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
    )
    answer = resp.content[0].text

    # Update profile from this turn (fire and forget — don't block the response)
    patch = extract_profile_update(user_message, answer)
    if patch:
        profile = apply_profile_update(profile, patch)
        profile.turn_count += 1
        save_profile(profile)

    return answer
Handling Conflicting Preferences
Users change their minds. A preference extracted six months ago may no longer be accurate. Three strategies handle staleness:
Recency weighting: when the same field is updated multiple times, prefer the most recent value rather than merging.
Confidence decay: tag each field with a timestamp; reduce its influence in the prompt injection after N days of inactivity.
Explicit override: honour direct user commands ('from now on, always use tables') immediately and with highest priority.
Privacy and Data Minimisation
User profiles are sensitive. Apply these principles before storing any profile data:
Store only what genuinely improves agent responses — not every observable fact.
Provide a clear deletion path: a /forget-me command that wipes the profile file and all derived preferences.
Never log profile contents to uncontrolled sinks (log files shipped to third parties, error trackers).
If operating under GDPR, treat the profile as personal data: document the legal basis, implement right-to-erasure, and restrict cross-border transfer.

def delete_profile(user_id: str) -> bool:
    """Permanently erase a user's profile. Irreversible."""
    path = PROFILE_DIR / f"{user_id}.json"
    if path.exists():
        path.unlink()
        return True
    return False
Testing Profile Logic

import pytest

def test_profile_roundtrip(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setattr("PROFILE_DIR", tmp_path)
    p = UserProfile(user_id="test", name="Alice", language="fr")
    save_profile(p)
    loaded = load_profile("test")
    assert loaded.name == "Alice"
    assert loaded.language == "fr"

def test_apply_update_merges_lists():
    profile = UserProfile(user_id="u1", domain_focus=["python"])
    patch   = {"domain_focus": ["finance", "python"]}   # "python" duplicate
    updated = apply_profile_update(profile, patch)
    assert updated.domain_focus == ["python", "finance"]  # deduped, order stable

def test_apply_update_ignores_unknown_keys():
    profile = UserProfile(user_id="u1")
    patch   = {"unknown_key": "value"}
    updated = apply_profile_update(profile, patch)
    assert not hasattr(updated, "unknown_key")

def test_to_prompt_block_contains_key_fields():
    profile = UserProfile(
        user_id="u1",
        name="Bob",
        expertise_level="expert",
        preferred_format="bullet",
    )
    block = profile.to_prompt_block()
    assert "Bob" in block
    assert "expert" in block
    assert "bullet" in block
A/B Testing Profile Adaptations

Before rolling out profile-driven personalisation to all users, measure whether it actually improves outcomes. A simple approach is to route a fraction of users to a control group (no profile injection) and measure response ratings, task completion rates, or session length.

import hashlib

def should_personalise(user_id: str, rollout_pct: int = 50) -> bool:
    """
    Deterministic, consistent assignment: the same user always lands
    in the same bucket for the duration of the experiment.
    Uses the last two hex digits of the user_id hash as a 0–99 bucket.
    """
    digest = hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest()
    bucket = int(digest[-2:], 16) % 100
    return bucket < rollout_pct


def chat_turn_with_experiment(
    user_id: str,
    user_message: str,
    history: list[dict],
    base_instructions: str,
) -> tuple[str, str]:
    """Returns (answer, variant) where variant is "personalised" or "control"."""
    if should_personalise(user_id):
        profile = load_profile(user_id)
        system  = build_system_prompt(base_instructions, profile)
        variant = "personalised"
    else:
        system  = base_instructions
        variant = "control"

    messages = history + [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return resp.content[0].text, variant
Only personalise when you have evidence it helps. A profile that injects stale or incorrect preferences can make responses worse than no profile at all.
Profile Versioning and Migration
As the agent evolves, the profile schema changes. Fields are added, renamed, or removed. Without a migration strategy, old profiles silently fail to load or inject wrong values.
Summary

User profiles turn a stateless LLM into an assistant that knows its users across sessions. The three-layer model — explicit fields set at onboarding, learned preferences extracted from conversation, and inferred traits derived from usage patterns — provides both reliability and depth. Automatic extraction via a fast model keeps the profile current without user effort. Injecting a compact profile block into every system prompt lets the agent adapt its communication style, detail level, and format preferences without any extra reasoning. Privacy-by-design — minimal storage, clear deletion, no uncontrolled logging — is not optional for profiles that accumulate personal data over time.
